coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent N, B-doped carbon nanodots performing as optical turn-off probes discerning Fe(II)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Azami, Mahsa
Valizadehderakhshan, Mehrab
Tukur, Panesun
Wei, Jianjun
coredata.dc:description: The interactions between metal ions and fluorescent probes are fundamentals for discerning the metal ions. Co-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs) have demonstrated the capacity to optically detect a wide variety of targets, including metal ions. In this work, the quenching of highly fluorescent, nitrogen (N) and boron (B) co-doped CNDs by Fe(II) ions was discovered and employed to differentiate Fe2+ in water samples and live cells. The fluorescence of the N,B-CNDs was quenched in a concentration-dependent manner with a need of very short reaction time (30 s), and the CNDs show good selectivity towards Fe(II) ions relative to other metal ions including Fe(III). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 1.8 nM and the linear response range was 7 nM to 1 μM. The recovery percentage was determined to be 97%, 101%, and 103% for spiked Fe(II) concentrations of 31, 125, and 1000 nM, respectively, in tap water. The N,B-CNDs were able to monitor the presence of Fe(II) in living cells using the optical imaging of Fe(II) ions, and the quenching in cells was completed within one hour. Regarding the quenching mechanism, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) procedure is proposed with support of cyclic voltammetry study on the CNDs upon addition of Fe(II). Overall, the N,B-CNDs promise as optical probes for the detection of Fe2+ in water and living cells, and demonstrated a satisfactory level of sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fe(II) ion detection
Quenching
Fluorescence-based probes
Live cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Screening of the sensing capability of N, B-doped and N, P-doped CNDs for Fe2+ and Fe3+ detection
Concentration dependency experiments and measurement of Fe2+ in real samples
Detection of Fe2+ in living cells
Selectivity of N, B-doped CNDs toward Fe2+ detection
Detection of Fe2+ in water and recovery experiments
Imaging detection of Fe2+ in living cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge support from the US NSF EiR research grant (award#: 1832134). This work was performed at the JSNN, a member of Southeastern Nanotechnology Infrastructure Corridor (SENIC) and National Nanotechnology Coordinated Infrastructure (NNCI), which is supported by the National Science Foundation (ECCS-2025462).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Counterplot of fluorescence results in terms of emission and excitation wavelengths for (a) N, B-doped, and (b) N, P-doped CNDs. And FTIR results of (c) N, B-doped and (d) N, P-doped CNDs.
(a) Fluorescence intensity of N, B-doped CNDs in the presence of different metal ions, and (b) the QA(%) of fluorescence of N, B-doped CNDs in presence of different metal ions at 50 μM.

Fluorescence intensity of N, B-doped CNDs (0.01 mg/mL) upon addition of different concentrations of Fe2+ (a). Linear plot of
F 0
- F
F
versus log[Fe2+] (b). The linear plot of
F 0
- F
F
versus Fe2+ concentration in the lower (c) and higher segment (d).
(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of N, B-doped CNDs with and without Fe2+ incubation. Tauc plot for band-gap energy calculations for (b) N, B-doped CNDs without, and (c) with Fe2+ addition.
Proposed mechanism for the quenching reaction between N, B-doped CNDs, and Fe2+ ions.
Cyclic voltammograms of Fe2+ incubated with different concentrations of N, B-doped CNDs.
The fluorescence images of EA.hy926 cells treated with CNDs in the absence of Fe2+ ions (a), in the presence of Fe2+ ions after 15 min (b), and 1 h (c).
Comparative study of fluorescent-based sensors used for Fe2+ detection reported in the literature.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent N, B-doped carbon nanodots performing as optical turn-off probes discerning Fe(II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience, Joint School of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering (JSNN), University of North Carolina at Greensboro, Greensboro, NC 27401, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint School of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering (JSNN)
University of North Carolina at Greensboro

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience, Joint School of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering (JSNN), University of North Carolina at Greensboro, Greensboro, NC 27401, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint School of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering (JSNN), North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University, Greensboro, NC 27401, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint School of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering (JSNN)
North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Joint School of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering (JSNN), North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University, Greensboro, NC 27401, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interactions between metal ions and fluorescent probes are fundamentals for discerning the metal ions. Co-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs) have demonstrated the capacity to optically detect a wide variety of targets, including metal ions. In this work, the quenching of highly fluorescent, nitrogen (N) and boron (B) co-doped CNDs by Fe(II) ions was discovered and employed to differentiate Fe2+ in water samples and live cells. The fluorescence of the N,B-CNDs was quenched in a concentration-dependent manner with a need of very short reaction time (30 s), and the CNDs show good selectivity towards Fe(II) ions relative to other metal ions including Fe(III). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 1.8 nM and the linear response range was 7 nM to 1 μM. The recovery percentage was determined to be 97%, 101%, and 103% for spiked Fe(II) concentrations of 31, 125, and 1000 nM, respectively, in tap water. The N,B-CNDs were able to monitor the presence of Fe(II) in living cells using the optical imaging of Fe(II) ions, and the quenching in cells was completed within one hour. Regarding the quenching mechanism, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) procedure is proposed with support of cyclic voltammetry study on the CNDs upon addition of Fe(II). Overall, the N,B-CNDs promise as optical probes for the detection of Fe2+ in water and living cells, and demonstrated a satisfactory level of sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ion detection is essential in a variety of scientific disciplines, including water treatment, medicine, ecology, and chemistry [1]. Determining the concentration levels of heavy metals and evaluating their health issues is vital in safeguarding potable water quality [2]. Iron concentration in municipal water is regulated to 0.3 mg/L by U.S. regulations [3] and the world health organization [4]. Numerous biological activities in the body need iron in different oxidation states (Fe2+ and Fe3+), including energy generation, oxygen delivery, DNA synthesis, etc. [5]. Fe2+ has been known as an essential nutrient for many cellular processes, and changes in its levels can disrupt cellular homeostasis and lead to disease [6]. This ion also participates in redox reactions and contributes to oxidative stress in cells [7], therefore monitoring Fe2+ levels can help understand the toxic mechanisms of these compounds and aid in the development of potential therapeutic strategies [6]. So far, many traditional techniques such as atomic absorption/emission spectrometry, ion chromatography, calorimetry, inductively-coupled plasma spectrometry, and spectrophotometry, have been applied to identify metal ions [8,9]. However, many of these detection methods—although possessing high sensitivity and accuracy—rely on the utilization of complex sample preparation and instrumentation [10].

Among emerging detection methods, “On-Off” fluorescence-based probes have gained the interest of researchers due to their facile synthesis, excellent selectivity, and sensitivity towards ions detection [11–13], including Fe2+ and Fe3+ [5,14]. To this end, CNDs have recently been identified as an ideal candidate for ion detection [15,16]. Radhakrishnan et al., have synthesized surface-passivated fluorescent carbon quantum dots and used them as promising turn-off fluorescence sensors for the detection of multiple analytes [17]. These surface-passivated CNDs were able to detect Cu2+, Pb2+, and Fe3+ ions in aqueous systems owing to their different binding abilities. CNDs have also been successfully used in Fe3+ detection. For instance, Arvapalli et al. synthesized highly fluorescent N-doped CNDs for the detection of Fe3+ in water, human plasma, and living cells [18]. The proposed mechanism was based on the charge transfer quenching between Fe3+ and CNDs. Wei et al designed and synthesized non-functionalized carbon nanodots and employed them for the detection of Fe2+ in water, bovine serum albumin, and live cells. In their sensor, the red fluorescence diminished after Fe2+ interaction and the LOD turned out to be as low as 51 nM [19]. Nevertheless, reports on the detection of Fe2+ due to its high oxidation potential are rare. Monitoring Fe2+ levels help understand the critical role of this metal ion in oxidative stress-related diseases and inform potential therapeutic strategies [20,21].

This work investigates the use of N, B-doped CNDs as a “Turn-Off” fluorescence probe for the detection of Fe2+ in water and living cells. We used a simple one-step closed vessel microwave synthesis method to prepare the N, B-doped CNDs and assessed its performance for Fe2+ detection. The proposed probe utilizes the intrinsic fluorescence properties of N, B-doped CNDs, which can be effectively quenched in the presence of Fe2+ ions. We believe that our study will provide a valuable tool for the detection of this ion in environmental and biological samples and can potentially find applications in various fields such as water quality monitoring, biomedical imaging, and disease diagnosis.

Citric acid, boric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), and phosphoric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH (Munich, Germany) and were used as received without further purification. KCl (ACROS Organics), CoCl2, FeCl2 (Alfa Aesar), FeCl3, CrCl3, AgNO3, CuCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, dialysis bags, gold electrode, Ag/AgCl reference electrode, platinum electrode (Fisher Scientific), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) media, EA.hy926 cell line (ATCC), pen/strep solution, and fetal bovine serum (Sigma Aldrich) were also used as received.

N, B-doped and N, P-doped CNDs were synthesized via a one-step closed-vessel microwave synthesis method. Citric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), phosphoric acid, and boric acid were used as the carbon, nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and boron (B) sources, respectively. To synthesize N, B-doped CNDs, 1 g citric acid and 1 mL EDA were dissolved in 1 mL deionized water (DW) and mixed to achieve a uniform solution. The solution was then transferred into a microwave synthesizer with the set points of temperature = 180 °C, time = 30 min, pressure = 100 Psi, and power = 300 Watts. Once the reaction was complete, the brownish semi-liquid product was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 mins to separate large particles. The supernatant was then transferred into a 1 kDa dialysis membrane and was dialyzed in DW for 48 h to further purify. After freeze-drying the retentate, a brown powder was obtained as N, B-doped CND product which was stored at room temperature for later use. The same procedure was repeated for the synthesis of N, P-doped CNDs, using 2 mL of phosphoric acid (22 N).

Different characterization techniques were used to determine the structural and optical features of CNDs. The size of the synthesized CNDs was measured using atomic force microscopy (AFM, MFP-3D Origin, Asylum). To prepare the AFM samples, a 0.002 mg/mL solution of each sample in DW was sonicated for 20 min and then drop-casted on a mica surface. To evaluate the formation of functional groups on the CNDs’ surface and elemental data analysis, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR – Varian 670) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS – Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi) were carried out. A solution of 10 mg/mL of CNDs was drop-casted and dried out for XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of the sensing capability of N, B-doped and N, P-doped CNDs for Fe2+ and Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
A simple experimental design was established to evaluate the detection selectivity and fluorescence quenching differences between N, B-doped and N, P-doped CNDs as Fe2+ and Fe3+ sensors. Solutions of 0.01 mg/mL N, B-doped and N, P-doped CNDs, respectively, were prepared and incubated with 50 μM solutions of Fe2+ and Fe3+ separately. FeCl2 and FeCl3 were used for the preparation of Fe2+ and Fe3+ solutions, respectively. For each of these experiments, the quenching ability (QA%) was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
QA
( % )
=
F 0
- F
F 0
× 100
where F0 and F are the fluorescence of CNDs samples at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm before and after incubation, respectively. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs with the addition of Fe2+ or Fe3+ ions was monitored at different time intervals of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 min. A 10 μL aliquot of each type of CNDs (0.01 mg/mL) was added to 100 μL of the desired metal ion and 890 μL of DW. As a control, a solution of 10 μL of CNDs (0.01 mg/mL) in 990 μL of DW was prepared, and the fluorescence was measured at the same time intervals as described.

Several metal ions including Fe3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Ag+ were selected and 100 μL of each at 50 μM was treated with 10 μL of N, B-doped CNDs (0.01 mg/mL). The QA% for each metal ion was calculated using Eq. (1). Meanwhile, the fluorescence changes in the presence of these metal ions with Fe2+ were tested to evaluate the competitive interference of each metal ion on the detection of Fe2+ by the CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration dependency experiments and measurement of Fe2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: A 30 μL aliquot of N, B-doped CNDs (0.01 mg/mL) was incubated with different concentrations of Fe2+ solution to investigate the concentration-dependent quenching efficiency of Fe2+ ions. The required volume of Fe2+ solution was taken and added to the 30 μL CNDs solutions to achieve 7, 15, 31, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 nM of Fe2+ in samples with the final volume of 3 mL for each experiment. Afterward, the QA% was calculated according to Eq. (1). Further, to analyze the efficiency of CNDs for Fe2+ detection in a real sample, tap water was spiked with known concentrations of Fe2+ [13]. The Fe2+ recovery rates were calculated using the standard curves. Next, to validate the accuracy of Fe2+ concentrations detected in tap water using N,B-doped CNDs as sensors, the inductively coupled plasma (ICP) spectroscopy was used. Three known concentrations of elemental Fe (0.05, 0.5, and 1 ppm) were prepared and their emission intensity was measured using ICP. A calibration curve was drawn and was used for validity experiments. Then the amount of Fe in tap water was measured using this method and compared to the results obtained using N, B-doped CNDs as sensor.

To further evaluate the effect of doping on the band-gap energy, Tauc plots were sketched using Eq. (2) [22].
(2)
(
α . h ν )
γ
= B
( h ν -
E g
)
where α, h, ν, Eg , and B are the absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, photon frequency, band-gap energy, and the proportionality constant, respectively. In this equation, γ = 2 due to direct allowed transitions. Due to the amorphous structure of CNDs, the γ factor was considered 0.5. Once plotted, a tangent line was drawn on the curve in the area of 3 to 4 eV on the X-axis. The intersection point is considered the apparent band-gap energy [23,24].

A three-electrode electrochemical cell system including a working gold electrode, 3 M Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and platinum counter electrode was used to conduct the cyclic voltammetry (CV). To run the experiment, different concentrations of nitrogen-purged CNDs solutions were added to the electrolyte solution (10 mM Fe2+). The samples were run at the scan rate of 20 mV s−1 at room temperature. The potential range was set between −0.2 and 0.9 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe2+ in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Human endothelial cells (EA.hy926) were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% pen/strep antibiotic solution. The cells were seeded on coverslips in a 12-well plate with a concentration of 104 cells per well and incubated for 24 h (37 °C and 5% CO2). Then the media was removed, and the incubation was continued with N, B-doped CNDs (0.5 mg/ml) for an additional 8 h. Then the cells were washed twice with PBS and the treatment was continued with 10 μM Fe2+ solution (dissolved in DMEM) for different time intervals of 10 min and 1 h. The cells were washed twice with 1x PBS and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 12 min. The cells were washed twice with 1x PBS and stained with MitoTracker green for 30 min (actin-staining). The coverslips were mounted to the glass slides and the imaging was conducted at 20X magnification using a confocal microscope (Zeiss Z1 Spinning Disk Confocal Microscope).

The AFM scans reveal a uniform distribution of both CNDs with an average height of 0.95 ± 0.05 nm and 0.66 ± 0.03 nm for N, B-doped and N, P-doped, respectively (Figure S1a,b). Fig. 1 a,b represent the fluorescence counterplots of CND samples (0.025 mg/mL) at different excitation wavelengths. Both CNDs exhibit significant excitation–dependent fluorescence intensities and the highest emission peak was observed for both at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. In the FTIR spectrum of N, B-doped CNDs (Fig. 1c) the appeared peaks at 1035, 1381, 1540, 1651, and 3258 cm−1 are assigned to B–C, C–H, CC, CO, and B–OH, respectively, and the N–H and O–H bending appeared in the range of 2800–3500 cm−1 [25]. In the N, P-doped CNDs’ FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d), the peaks that appeared at 940, 1390, 1545, 1657, and 2350 are assigned to P–H bending, C–H, CC, CO, and P–H, respectively [26]. The XPS full survey of N, B-doped and N, P-doped CNDs confirm the incorporation of B (peak at 189 eV for B 1s) and P (peaks at 189 eV for P 2 s and 133 eV for P 2p) in the CNDs structures (Fig. S2a,b). The more details of morphological, structural and optoelectronic properties of the N,P- and N-B-doped CNDs were included in our recent publication, and the fluorescence quantum yield is reported 25.1% and 25.8%, respectively [27].

The changes in the fluorescence intensities of N, B-doped and N, P-doped CNDs upon the addition of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions are monitored at 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 min intervals. As can be seen in Figure S3a, the quenching reaction in both CNDs solutions is completed within the first 30 s upon the addition of Fe2+. No significant changes in fluorescence were observed after the first 30 s. Therefore, in all experiments, fluorescence measurements were performed considering 30 s as the required time for complete quenching. Comparing two CNDs (Figure S3b) reveals that although the QA% of both Fe2+ and Fe3+ with N, P-doped CNDs is higher than that of N, B-doped CNDs, the N, B-CNDs exhibits a greater difference in QA% thus better selectivity between Fe2+ and Fe3+. For this reason, the following is more focused on the interactions of the N,B-CNDs with Fe2+ and Fe3+.

It was reported that Fe2+ has a higher affinity for complex formation than Fe3+ due to the electron configuration [28]. Fe2+ has an empty orbital that can accommodate an electron pair from a ligand, making complex formation more energetically favorable. Fe3+ on the other hand has a full orbital and less affinity for complex formation. This may explain why CNDs have a greater affinity for complex formation with Fe2+ than Fe3+ [29]. On the other hand, our recent findings of the effects of B-dopant and P-dopant on the CNDs can plausibly explain the more sensitive response of the N,B-CNDs to Fe2+ than Fe3+, respectively. Because the B-dopant CNDs demonstrate more influence on the surface functional groups and the surface states are primarily responsible for the fluorescence of B-doped CNDs [27], it is expected that the formation of the N,B-CNDs-[Fe2+]n complex has stronger effect on the surface states, thus inducing more sensitive in the fluorescence responses. More detailed investigation of the selectivity and photochemistry are presented below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N, B-doped CNDs toward Fe2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of N, B-doped CNDs toward the Fe2+ ions among other metal ions, several metal ions were selected and incubated with the CNDs. The changes in the fluorescence intensity of N, B-doped CNDs were monitored upon the addition of different metal ions (Fig. 2a). As shown in Fig. 2 b, the maximum fluorescence quenching occurred upon the addition of Fe2+ (∼42%) compared to the other metal ions (15% or lower). The interference of the same competitive metal ions in the Fe2+ ion detection was also investigated Fig. S4. Fluorescence quenching of the CNDs is evident in the presence of Fe2+ ions where the presence of other metal ions has mildly affected the quenching. Also, the last column of this figure refers to the fluorescence intensity background of Fe2+ ions without any CNDs.

Fig. 3 a presents the fluorescence spectra at different concentrations of Fe2+ with N, B-doped CNDs. A decrease in the fluorescence of CNDs is observed, which is attributed to the quenching effect that occurs upon the addition of Fe2+. To investigate the kinetic mechanism of the quenching reaction the Stern-Volmer model (Eq. (3) was used [30].
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]
where Ksv is the quenching constant and Q is the concentration of the quencher (Fe2+).

This equation was re-arranged and
F 0
- F
F
has the correlation to follow a logarithmic model (Fig. S5). Plotting the
F 0
- F
F
ratio versus the log[Fe2+] helps calculate the LOD (Fig. 3b) which is 1.8 nM (S/N = 3) within the detection range of 7 – 1000 nM. This linear relationship suggests an affinity reaction mechanism in which the quencher and the fluorescing molecule interact through a specific binding interaction [31]. Next, the logarithmic plot (Fig. S5) was split into two linear segments (Fig. 3c,d), and Ksv values calculated for lower and higher concentration linear segments were obtained to be 3.3 × 106 and 2 × 105 M−1, respectively. At lower Fe2+ concentrations (the first segment), the greater constant Ksv value indicates faster kinetics of the binding of CNDs to Fe2+ and a strong quenching effect due to a reaction between the fluorescing molecule and the quencher. The second segment of the plot with the lower Ksv indicates a weaker quenching effect. Based on this observation, it is possible that static quenching is occurring in this reaction in which an adduct is formed between the fluorescent species and the quencher. Fluorescing molecules are surrounded by the quencher at greater concentrations of the target ion (in the second segment), and additional quencher molecules have a diminished effect on quenching kinetics [32].

Fig. 4 a represents the UV–Vis spectrum of N, B-doped CNDs incubated with and without Fe2+. The UV–Vis spectrum of CNDs typically exhibits two significant peaks at approximately 240 and 350 nm due to π–π* transitions of CC and n–π* transitions of non-binding orbitals mostly belonging to CO, respectively [33,34]. Following incubation with Fe2+ ions (50 μM), both maximum peaks of N, B-doped CNDs (0.01 mg/mL) display a minor blue shift. The Fe2+ ions can interact with the N, B-doped CNDs and cause a shift in their electronic energy levels, which in turn affects the absorption spectra of the UV–Vis peaks. The Tauc plots driven from UV–Vis absorption spectra of N,B-doped CNDs before and after the addition of Fe2+ was drawn. The band-gap energies of CNDs without and with the addition of Fe2+ are obtained as 3.160 and 3.149, respectively (Fig. 4b,c). This phenomenon could confirm the complex formation of CNDs with Fe2+ through which the electronic structure of the N,B-doped CNDs is modified, resulting in changes in their optical properties. The interaction between the CNDs and Fe2+ ions induce charge transfer between the two species, leading to the formation of a new energy level within the band gap of the CNDs. This can be accounted for the changes in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels [23].

It is expected the involvement of specific surface sites of the N,B-CNDs interaction with the Fe2+ ions for the PET. Our study found that the B-dopant causes less −NH2 groups and the arise of NO3 − groups at the surface of N,B-CNDs, which strengthens the fluorescence by providing π − π conjugations through the electron resonance among NO bonds in its structure [27]. Another study demonstrates the occurrence of strong specific adsorption between Fe2+ (SA-Fe2+) and NO3 − within the inner Helmholtz plane and the SA- Fe2+ could facilitate the high-rate electroreduction of nitrate to ammonium [36]. Hence, the PET induced reduction of NO3 − to NH2 could happen and account for the more efficient fluorescence quenching than the N- or N,P-doped CNDs. However, the actual quenching mechanism would be more complicated due to involvement of more factors in the origin of the fluorescence [23].

To further verify the hypothesized charge transfer quenching mechanism, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed to reveal the availability of solvated free Fe2+. Fig. 6 presents the cyclic voltammograms associated with oxidation and reduction of Fe2+ ions before and after the inclusion of the CNDs into the system. The CV oxidation and reduction peaks of Fe2+ were visibly suppressed as the CND concentration was raised from 0 to 0.05 mg/mL. From these results, one can conclude that the N, B-doped CNDs-[Fe3+]n complex is formed thus decreasing the redox reaction due to the less availability of Fe2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe2+ in water and recovery experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Within the initial Fe2+ concentration range, three distinct levels at low (31 nM), medium (125 nM), and high (1000 nM) concentrations were prepared. The recovery percentage of known amounts of spiked Fe2+ in tap water samples using N, B-doped CNDs were calculated and reported in Table S1. Using the two segments obtained earlier, the Fe2+ recovery percentage was calculated as 97.09 ± 4.82, 101.98 ± 3.26, and 103.08 ± 1.78 for the 31 nM, 125 nM, and 1000 nM, respectively. Despite possessing a higher R2 value for the first segment (0.93) compared to the one for the second segment (0.91), standard errors of the recovery (%) results calculated using the second segments were lower. This means overall, N, B-doped CNDs as a sensor probe for detection of Fe2+ act more precisely in the higher concentrations of the selected range in this study.

Next, we measured the Fe2+ concentration in the tap water using the developed CND probes. The fluorescence intensity of 0.01 mg/mL N, B-doped CNDs solution in DW (F0) and in tap water (F) was measured and the
F 0
- F
F
was obtained. The Fe2+ concentration was calculated using the calibration equation of the second segment and the average value of three measurements are 0.108 ± 0.006 ppm. As a validity experiment, a calibration curve from Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) mass spectrometry measurements was drawn (Fig. S6) and the average quantity of Fe in three tap water samples was obtained as 0.115 ± 0.005 ppm, which is comparable with the results from the optical CND probes in this study. These results demonstrate the effectiveness using the N, B-doped CNDs for optical detection of Fe2+ in tap water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging detection of Fe2+ in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: As-synthesized N, B-doped CNDs could be employed for bioimaging cells due to their strong intrinsic blue fluorescence in cells. MitoTracker green was used to stain the mitochondria of the EA.hy926 cells. Fig. 7 a shows the MitoTrack green stained cells (left), bright blue fluorescence in cells with the absence of Fe2+ ions (middle), and the merged image (right). After 15 min of treatment with 10 μM Fe2+, moderate fluorescence quenching was observed (Fig. 7b), and it was almost completely quenched after 1 h treatment (Fig. 7c). The cells maintained intact after both the CNDs and Fe2+ treatment because insignificant changes were observed between Fig. 7a (left panel) and Fig. 7c (right panel) in terms of cell morphology. The observed blue fluorescence quenching suggests the effectiveness of using the CNDs as bioimaging probes for monitoring the uptake of Fe2+ in EA.hy926 cells, and potentially can be used for other living cells. The cytotoxicity of the CNDs in bioimaging, cellular effects and biological applications is very important. This work is undertaken and will be published elsewhere.

Most Fe2+ sensors presented in the literature either do not detect Fe2+ and Fe3+ selectively or require sophisticated components to increase selectivity [37,38]. For example, the CND sensors designed by Wei et al were able to detect Fe2+ ions, but their selectivity was limited by the shielding effects induced by tartaric and ascorbic acid present in the media [19]. Another CND-based sensor introduced by Xiao et al. was sensitive and selective and has a linear range at the μM scales [39]. The use of NaOH and other components raises biocompatibility concerns for biological applications. In another report, a highly sensitive CND-based sensor was proposed by Liu et al. that can detect both Fe2+ and Fe3+ as a total [40]. Table 1 summarizes recent research on fluorescent-based sensors used for Fe2+ detection.

We introduced highly fluorescent N, B-doped CNDs synthesized with a one-step microwave method providing high selectivity and sensitivity for detecting Fe2+ in water and live cells. The concentration-dependent in fluorescence drop is used as a quantitative signal transduction making these CNDs practical “Turn-Off” sensors for Fe2+ detection. The N,B-CND optical probe showed selectivity towards Fe2+ ions in the presence of other metal ions commonly found in water and living cells. The LOD for the CND optical sensing probe was calculated as 1.8 nM and the CND probe was validated for detecting Fe2+ in tap water. The N, B-doped CNDs can be taken up by endothelial cells for bioimaging with a vivid blue fluorescence. The CNDs' fluorescence in the cells was inhibited after being exposed to Fe2+ treatment, demonstrating their capability and sensitivity to monitoring this metal ion in living cells. Overall, the developed N, B-doped CNDs as a rapid responsive optical probe in Fe2+ detection offer improved sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, making it a promising tool in fields such as environmental monitoring, clinical diagnostics, and food safety testing.

Mahsa Azami: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mehrab Valizadehderakhshan: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Panesun Tukur: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jianjun Wei: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge support from the US NSF EiR research grant (award#: 1832134). This work was performed at the JSNN, a member of Southeastern Nanotechnology Infrastructure Corridor (SENIC) and National Nanotechnology Coordinated Infrastructure (NNCI), which is supported by the National Science Foundation (ECCS-2025462).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115111.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
